channel.title: Lore on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Lore on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Self-Consistent Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about self-consistent lore.

I’ve discussed realism and lore actually in a couple different videos. One I compared realism to fun and talked about how they’re not the same thing and I never consider them the same thing And for me fun trumps realism. So I’ll link that video in the description. But I’ve also talked about how realism isn’t even necessary. Um, it’s something that sometimes people comment on like this isn’t realistic and they’re playing a fantasy game with dragons and spells. I think part of the problem is realism gets conflated with consistency.

item.title: Let's Talk About Canon
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, let’s talk about Canon.

It’s a fun Friday. I want to talk about it, talk about different things, how I use it here, A lot of comments that I get about it And also, yeah, I see some boxes in the background.

Don’t worry about that, It’s that’s not what I’m talking about today. Just some boxes, some empty shelves.

Let’s move on, so. A lot of talking about canon on my channel and that’s what I want to talk about. Like, what is it? Who gets to decide it? What happens when people disagree? Wow, I see a lot of discussion of this.

item.title: Non-Expository Arcanum Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about non-expository Arcanum lore.

This is a companion video to the non-expository Fallout lore that I did a while back, where Fath Daniel asked: “What is Arcanum, non-expository lore” And I thought, “Yeah, I should do that because that makes sense” And I It’s something that it’s interesting. Um, it was harder for me to do this for Arcanum than for Fallout And because Fallout was a smaller game in sense of scope, number of NPCs, number of dialogues, cutscenes, in-game books — So it was harder for me to find Arcanum lore that had never been, that had never appeared in the game already. Like, there’s a cut scene that mentions the lore or an NPC talks about it at length, and we had several NPCs that could talk about a lot of lore that you may not know, because it was hidden behind a, speech skill, or you had to have a certain reactivity level with them, or you just never dove down. In their dialogue tree to find it. And then there were lots and lots and lots of books. So I tried to find lore for Arcanum.

item.title: Non-Expository Fallout Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, for a fun Friday, I wanted to talk about nonexpository Fallout lore.

Let me explain what I mean by that. First of all, I’m talking about lore from the first game only, not even from Fallout 2.. I’m talking about lore that the original team discussed among ourselves and decided was true, and then we made the game based on that. We never wrote this stuff down. In fact, there weren’t a lot of design documents back in the 90s. I think the vision document was one of the few truly laid out, organized, specific documents we wrote.

item.title: Fallout Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout lore exploration.

This comes from a really good question from Joseé Luis Lopez 7511, who asked: “What part of the Fallout universe needs to be expanded upon or what story or stories deserve to be continued? Perhaps take a bit from each game and weave the evolution of the formula”. This is an interesting question. Um, I debated whether to answer it because whenever I talk about Fallout, some people go crazy. But there’s a lot of me in Fallout. I’ve said this a lot.

item.title: Arcanum Lore Exploration
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. I want to talk to you today about Arcanum lore exploration.

What I mean by that is kind of a deep dive into some of the Arcanum lore that interests me. Now I did a video already on the sequel to Arcanum called Journey to the Center of Arcanum, and there’s a video about it. I’ll link it below, and in that there was this idea for combining magic and tech. There was an ore that let you make Tech ological items that you could magically imbue, and I didn’t like it then. And now, 25 years later, I absolutely hate the idea, and the reason for that is I think it takes a fundamental pillar of the Arcanum setting, which is Magic and Tech are diametrically opposed, not because they don’t like each other, not because they don’t get along and you need to find, you know, a substrate to get them, you know, happy to be together because they work diametrically oppositely.

item.title: The Number Of Vaults In Fallout
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the number of vaults in Fallout.

It’s Friday, fun day, so I want to talk about some fun. This is just me talking about It’s not canon — things I say aren’t Canon, but I thought it’ be fun to revisit this after my video months ago about the Vault being used as experiments, which I’ll get to. So the original design for vaults was that there was going to be a thousand, and the only reason this pin was even placed in the original game was when Leonard was doing the Vault suit. He asked me how many digits he’d need to keep on the back, because we had vault 13. But he wanted to know, you know, are they going to be Vault? You know 106, is there going to be Vault?

item.title: Lore Drift
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about lore drift.

I’ve seen people complain online, especially recently, about lore changing. Um, yeah, I’m talking Fallout lot, but I also I’m talking about this for a lot of ips. I mean, it’s not just Fallout people talk about this, it’s um, Star Trek, uh Dune. For me it was the Conan stuff when Conan went from being a book to a movie. And I want to talk about all this.

item.title: Game Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game lore.

I want to answer a question from armored spaz, who asks: how do you keep players informed about the game world, and how could you, how could the game be designed to make the player want to find out more about the game? I think this is a really good question. I almost didn’t answer it though, because it kind of moves into the narrative design territory that I have said in the past, I’m not that good at. But I think I’m good at thinking about narrative design. I’m just not good at implementing narrative design, so let’s talk about that also.

item.title: Fallout: Radiation vs FEV
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about radiation and the fev virus in Fallout and before I start I just want to say

this is my opinion. But if the opinion I had back then — and I even looked at my notes — I’m like, yep, that’s what I thought and it doesn’t really matter what I think because Canon is now set by Bethesda. But I also want to point out that even back then there was disagreement on the team over some of these, especially Goulds, which I will explain last, because I wanted, I want to walk through what my take on all of it was and what I had planned to do and make the and make Canon, starting in Fallout 2 and later.

item.title: The True Purpose of Vaults in Fallout
item.description: Hi everyone, it’s me Tim. Wow, you are filling up my comments with so many good ideas for topics that I can’t even really keep up. I had planned today, uh, to either talk about Bloodlines, vampire Bloodlines and the cut content and some of our plans for the sequels, and then I thought, no, no, I’m gonna talk about music, and not just the great music in Fallout, but arcanum’s music and vampires music and the fact that I’m sad each and every day that I got to work with the composer, Johan Johansson.
